XJTU System Optimization and Scheduling Course Assignments

发表于 2022-11-29 分类于 Study Valine：
本文字数： 40k 阅读时长 ≈ 37 分钟

西安交通大学研究生《系统优化与调度》课程作业

Assignment 1

(1)1.1.2

(a)

f(x,y)=(x2−4)2+y2∇f(x,y)=(4x3−16,2y)∇2f(x,y)=(12x2−16002)f(x,y)=(x^2-4)^2+y^2\\\\ \nabla f(x,y)=(4x^3-16,2y)\\\\ \nabla^2f(x,y)= \begin{pmatrix} 12x^2-16 & 0\\ 0 & 2 \end{pmatrix} f(x,y)=(x2−4)2+y2∇f(x,y)=(4x3−16,2y)∇2f(x,y)=(12x2−160​02​)

so there are three stationary points: (2, 0) (-2, 0) and (0, 0)

the Hesse Matrix is positive semi-definite only when ∣x∣>43|x|>\frac43∣x∣>34​

so points (2, 0) (-2, 0) are local minimum points, point (0, 0) is not a local minimum.

f(2,0)=f(−2,0)=0f(2,0)=f(-2,0)=0 f(2,0)=f(−2,0)=0

so (2,0) and (-2,0) are global minimum points of f

let g(x,y)=−f(x,y)=−(x2−4)2−y2g(x,y)=-f(x,y)=-(x^2-4)^2-y^2g(x,y)=−f(x,y)=−(x2−4)2−y2, it is easy to know that Hesse matrix is not positive semi-definite when x=0,y=0x=0,y=0x=0,y=0. So the point(0,0) is not the minimum of g(x,y)g(x,y)g(x,y), which means it is also not the local maximum of f(x,y)f(x,y)f(x,y).

(b)

f(x,y)=12x2+xcos y∇f(x,y)=(x+cos y,−xsin y)f(x,y)=\frac12x^2+xcos\ y\\\\ \nabla f(x,y)=(x+cos\ y,-xsin\ y) f(x,y)=21​x2+xcos y∇f(x,y)=(x+cos y,−xsin y)

−xsin y=0-xsin\ y=0−xsin y=0 only if x=0x=0x=0 or y=kπ (k∈Z)y=k\pi\ (k\in Z)y=kπ (k∈Z)

if x=0x=0x=0, x+cos y=0x+cos\ y=0x+cos y=0 only if y=π2+kπ (k∈Z)y=\frac\pi2+k\pi\ (k\in Z)y=2π​+kπ (k∈Z)

if y=kπ (k∈Z)y=k\pi\ (k\in Z)y=kπ (k∈Z), x+cos y=0x+cos\ y=0x+cos y=0 only if x=−(−1)kx=-(-1)^kx=−(−1)k

so the stationary points of f(x,y)f(x,y)f(x,y) are (0,π2+kπ)(0, \frac\pi2+k\pi)(0,2π​+kπ) and (−(−1)k,kπ)(-(-1)^k,k\pi)(−(−1)k,kπ) (k∈Z)(k\in Z)(k∈Z)

∇2f(x,y)=(1−sin y−sin y−xcos y)\nabla^2f(x,y)= \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -sin\ y\\ -sin\ y & -xcos\ y \end{pmatrix} ∇2f(x,y)=(1−sin y​−sin y−xcos y​)

∣(1)∣>0∣∇2f(x,y)∣=−xcos y−sin2 y|(1)|>0\\ |\nabla^2f(x,y)|=-xcos\ y-sin^2\ y ∣(1)∣>0∣∇2f(x,y)∣=−xcos y−sin2 y

for points (0,π2+kπ)(0, \frac\pi2+k\pi)(0,2π​+kπ),

∣∇2f(x,y)∣=0−1=−1<0|\nabla^2f(x,y)|=0-1=-1<0 ∣∇2f(x,y)∣=0−1=−1<0

for points (−(−1)k,kπ)(-(-1)^k,k\pi)(−(−1)k,kπ),

∣∇2f(x,y)∣=−1−0=−1<0|\nabla^2f(x,y)|=-1-0=-1<0 ∣∇2f(x,y)∣=−1−0=−1<0

so all the stationary points are not local minimum points.

function f(x,y)f(x,y)f(x,y) have no local minima

©

f(x,y)=sin x+sin y+sin (x+y)∇f(x,y)=(cos x+cos (x+y),cos y+cos (x+y))∇2f(x,y)=(−sin x−sin(x+y)−sin(x+y)−sin (x+y)−sin y−sin(x+y))f(x,y)=sin\ x+sin\ y+sin\ (x+y)\\\\ \nabla f(x,y)=(cos\ x+cos\ (x+y),cos\ y+cos\ (x+y))\\\\ \nabla^2f(x,y)= \begin{pmatrix} -sin\ x-sin(x+y) & -sin(x+y)\\ -sin\ (x+y) & -sin\ y-sin(x+y) \end{pmatrix} f(x,y)=sin x+sin y+sin (x+y)∇f(x,y)=(cos x+cos (x+y),cos y+cos (x+y))∇2f(x,y)=(−sin x−sin(x+y)−sin (x+y)​−sin(x+y)−sin y−sin(x+y)​)

let \left\{ \begin{array}{**lr**} cos\ x+cos\ (x+y)=0&(1)\\ cos\ y+cos\ (x+y)=0&(2) \end{array} \right.

(1)-(2): cos x−cos y=0cos\ x-cos\ y=0cos x−cos y=0, so y=x or y=2π−xy=x\ or\ y=2\pi-xy=x or y=2π−x

if y=xy=xy=x, (1) equals to cos x+cos 2x=0cos\ x+cos\ 2x=0cos x+cos 2x=0,

using double angle formula: cos x+2cos2 x−1=0cos\ x+2cos^2\ x-1=0cos x+2cos2 x−1=0

solution: cos x=12 , −1cos\ x=\frac12\ ,\ -1cos x=21​ , −1, x=π3,π,53πx=\frac\pi3,\pi,\frac53\pix=3π​,π,35​π

if y=2π−xy=2\pi-xy=2π−x, (1) equals to cos x+1=0cos\ x+1=0cos x+1=0

solution: cos x=−1cos\ x=-1cos x=−1, x=πx=\pix=π

The stationary points are (π3,π3),(π,π),(53π,53π)(\frac\pi3,\frac\pi3),(\pi,\pi),(\frac53\pi,\frac53\pi)(3π​,3π​),(π,π),(35​π,35​π).

∣∇2f(x,y)∣=sin xsin y+(sin x+sin y)sin (x+y)|\nabla^2f(x,y)|=sin\ xsin\ y+(sin\ x+sin\ y)sin\ (x+y) ∣∇2f(x,y)∣=sin xsin y+(sin x+sin y)sin (x+y)

for point (π3,π3)(\frac\pi3,\frac\pi3)(3π​,3π​):

∣(−sin x−sin(x+y))∣=−3∣∇2f(x,y)∣=94|(-sin\ x-sin(x+y))|=-\sqrt 3\\ |\nabla^2f(x,y)|=\frac94 ∣(−sin x−sin(x+y))∣=−3​∣∇2f(x,y)∣=49​

its Hesse Matrix is negative definite, so point (π3,π3)(\frac\pi3,\frac\pi3)(3π​,3π​) is a local maximum point, max value is 323\frac32\sqrt323​3​

for point (π,π)(\pi,\pi)(π,π):

∣(−sin x−sin(x+y))∣=0∣∇2f(x,y)∣=0|(-sin\ x-sin(x+y))|=0\\ |\nabla^2f(x,y)|=0 ∣(−sin x−sin(x+y))∣=0∣∇2f(x,y)∣=0

this point is neither a local maximum nor a local minimum.

for point (53π,53π)(\frac53\pi,\frac53\pi)(35​π,35​π):

∣(−sin x−sin(x+y))∣=3∣∇2f(x,y)∣=−34|(-sin\ x-sin(x+y))|=\sqrt3\\ |\nabla^2f(x,y)|=-\frac34 ∣(−sin x−sin(x+y))∣=3​∣∇2f(x,y)∣=−43​

this point is neither a local maximum nor a local minimum.

(d)

f(x,y)=(y−x2)2−x2∇f(x,y)=(4x3−4xy−2x,−2x2+2y)∇2f(x,y)=(12x2−4y−2−4x−4x2)f(x,y)=(y-x^2)^2-x^2\\\\ \nabla f(x,y)=(4x^3-4xy-2x,-2x^2+2y)\\\\ \nabla^2f(x,y)= \begin{pmatrix} 12x^2-4y-2 & -4x\\ -4x & 2 \end{pmatrix} f(x,y)=(y−x2)2−x2∇f(x,y)=(4x3−4xy−2x,−2x2+2y)∇2f(x,y)=(12x2−4y−2−4x​−4x2​)

let \left\{ \begin{array}{**lr**} 4x^3-4xy-2x=0\\ -2x^2+2y=0 \end{array} \right.

Thus the stationary point is (0,0)(0,0)(0,0), for this point:

∣(12x2−4y−2)∣=−2∣∇2f(x,y)∣=−4|(12x^2-4y-2)|=-2\\ |\nabla^2f(x,y)|=-4 ∣(12x2−4y−2)∣=−2∣∇2f(x,y)∣=−4

its Hesse Matrix is neither positive definite nor negative definite, so this stationary point is neither a local maximum nor a local minimum.

(e)

Since the conclusion of (d)——the stationary point is neither a local maximum nor a local minimum, so the minimum point could only exist on the boundary.

Consider y=−1y=-1y=−1, then f(x,y)=(−1−x2)2−x2=x4+x2+1f(x,y)=(-1-x^2)^2-x^2=x^4+x^2+1f(x,y)=(−1−x2)2−x2=x4+x2+1.

In this case, the minimum point is (0,−1)(0,-1)(0,−1) and the minimum is 1

Consider y=1y=1y=1, then f(x,y)=(1−x2)2−x2=x4−3x2+1f(x,y)=(1-x^2)^2-x^2=x^4-3x^2+1f(x,y)=(1−x2)2−x2=x4−3x2+1

In this case, the minimum points are (62,1),(−62,1)(\frac{\sqrt6}2,1), (-\frac{\sqrt6}2,1)(26​​,1),(−26​​,1), and the minimum is −54-\frac54−45​.

Summarizing, with the constraint −1≤y≤1-1\leq y\leq 1−1≤y≤1 on yyy, there exists global minimum and the global minimum points are (62,1),(−62,1)(\frac{\sqrt6}2,1), (-\frac{\sqrt6}2,1)(26​​,1),(−26​​,1), the global minimum is −54-\frac54−45​.

(2)1.1.6

suppose x=(a,b),yi=(ai,bi)x=(a,b),y_i=(a_i,b_i)x=(a,b),yi​=(ai​,bi​), then the problem could transform to:

min ∑i=1mwi(a−ai)2+(b−bi)2min\ \sum_{i=1}^mw_i\sqrt{(a-a_i)^2+(b-b^i)^2} min i=1∑m​wi​(a−ai​)2+(b−bi)2​

suppose f(a,b)=∑i=1mwi(a−ai)2+(b−bi)2f(a,b)=\sum_{i=1}^mw_i\sqrt{(a-a_i)^2+(b-b^i)^2}f(a,b)=∑i=1m​wi​(a−ai​)2+(b−bi)2​, then:

∇f(x,y)=(∑i=1mwia−ai(a−ai)2+(b−bi)2,∑i=1mwib−bi(a−ai)2+(b−bi)2)∇2f(a,b)=(∑i=1mwi2(b−bi)2((a−ai)2+(b−bi)2)3∑i=1mwi−2(a−ai)(b−bi)((a−ai)2+(b−bi)2)3∑i=1mwi−2(a−ai)(b−bi)((a−ai)2+(b−bi)2)3∑i=1mwi2(a−ai)2((a−ai)2+(b−bi)2)3)\nabla f(x,y)=(\sum_{i=1}^mw_i\frac{a-a_i}{\sqrt{(a-a_i)^2+(b-b^i)^2}},\sum_{i=1}^mw_i\frac{b-b_i}{\sqrt{(a-a_i)^2+(b-b^i)^2}})\\\\ \nabla^2f(a,b)= \begin{pmatrix} \sum_{i=1}^mw_i\frac{2(b-b_i)^2}{\sqrt{((a-a_i)^2+(b-b^i)^2)^3}} & \sum_{i=1}^mw_i\frac{-2(a-a_i)(b-b_i)}{\sqrt{((a-a_i)^2+(b-b^i)^2)^3}}\\ \sum_{i=1}^mw_i\frac{-2(a-a_i)(b-b_i)}{\sqrt{((a-a_i)^2+(b-b^i)^2)^3}} & \sum_{i=1}^mw_i\frac{2(a-a_i)^2}{\sqrt{((a-a_i)^2+(b-b^i)^2)^3}} \end{pmatrix} ∇f(x,y)=(i=1∑m​wi​(a−ai​)2+(b−bi)2​a−ai​​,i=1∑m​wi​(a−ai​)2+(b−bi)2​b−bi​​)∇2f(a,b)=⎝⎜⎛​∑i=1m​wi​((a−ai​)2+(b−bi)2)3​2(b−bi​)2​∑i=1m​wi​((a−ai​)2+(b−bi)2)3​−2(a−ai​)(b−bi​)​​∑i=1m​wi​((a−ai​)2+(b−bi)2)3​−2(a−ai​)(b−bi​)​∑i=1m​wi​((a−ai​)2+(b−bi)2)3​2(a−ai​)2​​⎠⎟⎞​

let ∇f(a,b)=0\nabla f(a,b)=0∇f(a,b)=0, then the stationary point is (∑i=1mwiai∑i=1mwi,∑i=1mwibi∑i=1mwi)(\frac{\sum_{i=1}^mw_ia_i}{\sum_{i=1}^mwi},\frac{\sum_{i=1}^mw_ib_i}{\sum_{i=1}^mwi})(∑i=1m​wi∑i=1m​wi​ai​​,∑i=1m​wi∑i=1m​wi​bi​​)

and ∇2f(a,b)\nabla^2 f(a,b)∇2f(a,b) is positive definite in the point, so it is the global minimum.

(b)

there is only one stationary point in this question, so the solution is unique

©

let the plane points on be the reference level

suppose the length of string in weight iii is lil_ili​, so the problem is:

min ∑i=1mwi(∣∣x−y∣∣−li)=∑i=1mwi∣∣x−y∣∣−∑i=1mwi⋅limin\ \sum_{i=1}^mw_i(||x-y||-l_i)=\sum_{i=1}^mw_i||x-y||-\sum_{i=1}^mw_i·l_i min i=1∑m​wi​(∣∣x−y∣∣−li​)=i=1∑m​wi​∣∣x−y∣∣−i=1∑m​wi​⋅li​

and we can see that ∑i=1mwi⋅li\sum_{i=1}^mw_i·l_i∑i=1m​wi​⋅li​ is constant, so the problem could transform to:

∑i=1mwi∣∣x−y∣∣\sum_{i=1}^mw_i||x-y|| i=1∑m​wi​∣∣x−y∣∣

which is same as the problem in (a).

(3) 1.2.1

(a)

f(x,y)=3x2+y4∇f(x,y)=(6x,4y3)f(x,y)=3x^2+y^4\\\\ \nabla f(x,y)=(6x,4y^3) f(x,y)=3x2+y4∇f(x,y)=(6x,4y3)

the start point is (1,−2)(1,-2)(1,−2), so the descend direction is (−6,32)′(-6,32)'(−6,32)′. Then choose mmm

at first, m=0,α=s=1m=0,\alpha=s=1m=0,α=s=1

f(1,2)−f(−5,30)=19−810075=−810056≤−0.1∗1∗−1060=106f(1,2)-f(-5,30)=19-810075=-810056\leq-0.1*1*-1060=106 f(1,2)−f(−5,30)=19−810075=−810056≤−0.1∗1∗−1060=106

then m=1，α=α∗β=0.5m=1，\alpha=\alpha*\beta=0.5m=1，α=α∗β=0.5

f(1,−2)−f(−2,14)=19−38428=−38409≤−0.1∗0.5∗−1060=53f(1,-2)-f(-2,14)=19-38428=-38409\leq -0.1*0.5*-1060=53 f(1,−2)−f(−2,14)=19−38428=−38409≤−0.1∗0.5∗−1060=53

then m=2,α=α∗β=0.25m=2,\alpha=\alpha*\beta=0.25m=2,α=α∗β=0.25

f(1,−2)−f(−0.5,6)=19−1296.75=−1277.75≤−0.1∗0.25∗−1060=26.5f(1,-2)-f(-0.5,6)=19-1296.75=-1277.75\leq -0.1*0.25*-1060=26.5 f(1,−2)−f(−0.5,6)=19−1296.75=−1277.75≤−0.1∗0.25∗−1060=26.5

then m=2,α=α∗β=0.125m=2,\alpha=\alpha*\beta=0.125m=2,α=α∗β=0.125

f(1,−2)−f(0.25,2)=19−16.1875≤−0.1∗0.125∗−1060=13.25f(1,-2)-f(0.25,2)=19-16.1875\leq -0.1*0.125*-1060=13.25 f(1,−2)−f(0.25,2)=19−16.1875≤−0.1∗0.125∗−1060=13.25

then m=3,α=α∗β=0.0625m=3,\alpha=\alpha*\beta=0.0625m=3,α=α∗β=0.0625

f(1,−2)−f(0.625,0)=19−1.171875=17.828≥−0.1∗0.0625∗−1060=6.625f(1,-2)-f(0.625,0)=19-1.171875=17.828\geq -0.1*0.0625*-1060=6.625 f(1,−2)−f(0.625,0)=19−1.171875=17.828≥−0.1∗0.0625∗−1060=6.625

so the first stepsize is 0.0625, after the iteration, the point changes to (0.625,0)(0.625, 0)(0.625,0)

(b)

when m=0,α=s=1m=0,\alpha=s=1m=0,α=s=1, the result is above.

then m=1，α=α∗β=0.1m=1，\alpha=\alpha*\beta=0.1m=1，α=α∗β=0.1

f(1,−2)−f(0.4,1.2)=19−2.5536=16.4464≥−0.1∗0.1∗1060=−10.6f(1,-2)-f(0.4,1.2)=19-2.5536=16.4464\geq -0.1*0.1*1060=-10.6 f(1,−2)−f(0.4,1.2)=19−2.5536=16.4464≥−0.1∗0.1∗1060=−10.6

so the first stepsize is 0.1, after the iteration, the point changes to (0.4,1.2)(0.4, 1.2)(0.4,1.2)

©

∇2f(x,y)=(60012y2)\nabla^2f(x,y)= \begin{pmatrix} 6&0\\ 0&12y^2 \end{pmatrix} ∇2f(x,y)=(60​012y2​)

at point (1,−2)(1,-2)(1,−2), the Hesse Matrix is:

(60048)\begin{pmatrix} 6&0\\ 0&48 \end{pmatrix} (60​048​)

its inverse matrix is:

(1600148)\begin{pmatrix} \frac16&0\\ 0&\frac1{48} \end{pmatrix} (61​0​0481​​)

so the descend direction is:

d=−(1600148)(6−32)=(−123)d=-\begin{pmatrix} \frac16&0\\ 0&\frac1{48} \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 6\\-32 \end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix} -1\\\frac23 \end{pmatrix} d=−(61​0​0481​​)(6−32​)=(−132​​)

then choose the stepsize:

at first, m=0,α=s=1m=0,\alpha=s=1m=0,α=s=1

f(1,−2)−f(0,−43)=19−3.16=15.84≥−0.1∗1∗1060=−106f(1,-2)-f(0,-\frac43)=19-3.16=15.84\geq -0.1*1*1060=-106 f(1,−2)−f(0,−34​)=19−3.16=15.84≥−0.1∗1∗1060=−106

so the first stepsize is 1, and the point changes to (0,−43)(0,-\frac43)(0,−34​)

Comparing with (a), the computing cost of Newton’s method is less

(4) 1.2.6

(a)

PROOF:

∇f(x)=Qx−b′∣∣∇f(x)−∇f(y)∣∣=∣∣Qx−Qy∣∣≤∣∣Q∣∣⋅∣∣x−y∣∣=L⋅∣∣x−y∣∣\nabla f(x)=Qx-b'\\\\ ||\nabla f(x)-\nabla f(y)||=||Qx-Qy||\leq ||Q||·||x-y||=L·||x-y|| ∇f(x)=Qx−b′∣∣∇f(x)−∇f(y)∣∣=∣∣Qx−Qy∣∣≤∣∣Q∣∣⋅∣∣x−y∣∣=L⋅∣∣x−y∣∣

(b)

PROOF:

xk+1=xk−sD∇f(xk)=xk−sD(Qxk−b′)xk+1=(I−sDQ)xk+sDb′x^{k+1}=x^k-sD\nabla f(x^k)=x^k-sD(Qx^k-b')\\ x^{k+1}=(I-sDQ)x^k+sDb' xk+1=xk−sD∇f(xk)=xk−sD(Qxk−b′)xk+1=(I−sDQ)xk+sDb′

ICBS:

xk+1+C=A(xk+C)where  A=I−sDQ,B=sDb′,C=(A−I)−1Bx^{k+1}+C=A(x^k+C)\\where\ \ A=I-sDQ,B=sDb', C=(A-I)^{-1}B xk+1+C=A(xk+C)where  A=I−sDQ,B=sDb′,C=(A−I)−1B

by iteration:

xk+1+C=A(xk+C)=Ak+1(x0+C)xk+1=A(xk+C)=Ak+1(x0+C)−Cx^{k+1}+C=A(x^k+C)=A^{k+1}(x^0+C)\\ x^{k+1}=A(x^k+C)=A^{k+1}(x^0+C)-C xk+1+C=A(xk+C)=Ak+1(x0+C)xk+1=A(xk+C)=Ak+1(x0+C)−C

then:

xk+1=(I−sDQ)k+1(x0−Q−1b′)+Q−1b′xk+1−x∗=(I−sDQ)k+1(x0−Q−1b′)+Q−1b′−Q−1b′xk+1−x∗=(I−sDQ)k+1(x0−Q−1b′)x^{k+1}=(I-sDQ)^{k+1}(x^0-Q^{-1}b')+Q^{-1}b'\\ x^{k+1}-x^*=(I-sDQ)^{k+1}(x^0-Q^{-1}b')+Q^{-1}b'-Q^{-1}b'\\ x^{k+1}-x^*=(I-sDQ)^{k+1}(x^0-Q^{-1}b')\\ xk+1=(I−sDQ)k+1(x0−Q−1b′)+Q−1b′xk+1−x∗=(I−sDQ)k+1(x0−Q−1b′)+Q−1b′−Q−1b′xk+1−x∗=(I−sDQ)k+1(x0−Q−1b′)

so:

lim⁡k→∞(xk+1−x∗)=lim⁡k→∞(I−sDQ)k+1(x0−Q−1b′)\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}(x^{k+1}-x^*)=\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}(I-sDQ)^{k+1}(x^0-Q^{-1}b') k→∞lim​(xk+1−x∗)=k→∞lim​(I−sDQ)k+1(x0−Q−1b′)

∀s∈(0,2/L),(I−sDQ)<1\forall s\in(0,2/L),\quad (I-sDQ)<1 ∀s∈(0,2/L),(I−sDQ)<1

then:

lim⁡k→∞(I−sDQ)k+1(x0−Q−1b′)=0lim⁡k→∞(xk+1−x∗)=0\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}(I-sDQ)^{k+1}(x^0-Q^{-1}b')=0\\ \lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}(x^{k+1}-x^*)=0 k→∞lim​(I−sDQ)k+1(x0−Q−1b′)=0k→∞lim​(xk+1−x∗)=0

Summarizing, the method converges to x∗=Q−1b′x^*=Q^{-1}b'x∗=Q−1b′ for every starting point x0x^0x0 if and only if s∈(0,2/L)s\in(0,2/L)s∈(0,2/L).

(5) 1.3.1

Q=(10.99950.99951)Q=\begin{pmatrix} 1&0.9995\\ 0.9995&1 \end{pmatrix} Q=(10.9995​0.99951​)

M=largest eigenvalue of Q=1.9995m=smallest eigenvalue of Q=0.0005f(xk+1)f(xk)≤(M−mM+m)2=0.999M=largest\ eigenvalue\ of\ Q= 1.9995\\\\ m=smallest\ eigenvalue\ of\ Q= 0.0005\\\\ \frac{f(x^{k+1})}{f(x^{k})}\leq (\frac{M-m}{M+m})^2= 0.999 M=largest eigenvalue of Q=1.9995m=smallest eigenvalue of Q=0.0005f(xk)f(xk+1)​≤(M+mM−m​)2=0.999

Let vmv_mvm​and vMv_MvM​ be the normalized eigenvectors of Q corresponding

to m and M, respectively, and let

x0=smvm±sMvM,s∈Rx^0=\frac smv_m\pm\frac sMv_M, s\in R x0=ms​vm​±Ms​vM​,s∈R

Then after the first iteration using the line minimization stepsize

rule,（i.e., a stepsizeα=2M+m\alpha=\frac2{M+m}α=M+m2​ ), we get

x1=M−mM+m(smvm∓sMvM)=M−mM+mx0x^1=\frac{M-m}{M+m}(\frac smv_m\mp\frac sMv_M)=\frac{M-m}{M+m}x^0 x1=M+mM−m​(ms​vm​∓Ms​vM​)=M+mM−m​x0

Therefore, starting the iterations withx0x^0x0 , we have for all k

f(xk+1)f(xk)=(M−mM+m)2\frac{f(x^{k+1})}{f(x^{k})}= (\frac{M-m}{M+m})^2 f(xk)f(xk+1)​=(M+mM−m​)2

(6) 1.3.4

f(x)=12(x−x∗)′Q(x−x∗)∇f(x)=Q(x−x∗)f(x)=\frac12(x-x^*)'Q(x-x^*)\\\\ \nabla f(x)=Q(x-x^*) f(x)=21​(x−x∗)′Q(x−x∗)∇f(x)=Q(x−x∗)

then:

xk+1=xk−s(Q(xk−x∗)+ek)xk+1=xk(I−sQ)+sQx∗+sekxk+1−x∗=(xk−x∗)(I−sQ)+sekx^{k+1}=x^k-s(Q(x^k-x^*)+e^k)\\ x^{k+1}=x^k(I-sQ)+sQx^*+se^k\\ x^{k+1}-x^*=(x^k-x^*)(I-sQ)+se^k xk+1=xk−s(Q(xk−x∗)+ek)xk+1=xk(I−sQ)+sQx∗+sekxk+1−x∗=(xk−x∗)(I−sQ)+sek

since for any AB,∣∣A⋅B∣∣≤∣∣A∣∣⋅∣∣B∣∣A B, ||A·B||\leq||A||·||B||AB,∣∣A⋅B∣∣≤∣∣A∣∣⋅∣∣B∣∣

∣∣xk+1−x∗∣∣≤∣∣(xk−x∗)(I−sQ)∣∣+s∣∣ek∣∣||x^{k+1}-x^*||\leq ||(x^k-x^*)(I-sQ)||+s||e^k|| ∣∣xk+1−x∗∣∣≤∣∣(xk−x∗)(I−sQ)∣∣+s∣∣ek∣∣

then:

∣∣xk+1−x∗∣∣≤∣∣xk−x∗∣∣⋅q+sδ||x^{k+1}-x^*||\leq ||x^k-x^*||·q+s\delta ∣∣xk+1−x∗∣∣≤∣∣xk−x∗∣∣⋅q+sδ

by the iteration,

∣∣xk−x0∣∣≤qk∣∣x0−xk∣∣+sδ(1+q+q2+...+qk−1)||x^k-x^0||\leq q^k||x0-x^k||+s\delta(1+q+q^2+...+q^{k-1}) ∣∣xk−x0∣∣≤qk∣∣x0−xk∣∣+sδ(1+q+q2+...+qk−1)

so:

∣∣xk−x0∣∣≤qk∣∣x0−xk∣∣+sδ1−q||x^k-x^0||\leq q^k||x0-x^k||+\frac{s\delta}{1-q} ∣∣xk−x0∣∣≤qk∣∣x0−xk∣∣+1−qsδ​

i.e.

∣∣xk−x0∣∣≤sδ1−q+qk∣∣x0−xk∣∣||x^k-x^0||\leq \frac{s\delta}{1-q}+q^k||x0-x^k|| ∣∣xk−x0∣∣≤1−qsδ​+qk∣∣x0−xk∣∣

(7) 1.4.1

In Newton’s Method, we have:

xk+1=xk−αk(∇2f(xk))−1∇f(xk)x^{k+1}=x^k-\alpha^k(\nabla^2f(x^k))^{-1}\nabla f(x^k) xk+1=xk−αk(∇2f(xk))−1∇f(xk)

consider x=Syx=Syx=Sy, then:

Syk+1=Syk−S−2αk(∇2f(Syk))−1S∇f(Syk)yk+1=yk−S−1αk(∇2f(Syk))−1∇f(Syk)Sy^{k+1}=Sy^k-S^{-2}\alpha^k(\nabla^2f(Sy^k))^{-1}S\nabla f(Sy^k)\\\\ y^{k+1}=y^k-S^{-1}\alpha^k(\nabla^2f(Sy^k))^{-1}\nabla f(Sy^k) Syk+1=Syk−S−2αk(∇2f(Syk))−1S∇f(Syk)yk+1=yk−S−1αk(∇2f(Syk))−1∇f(Syk)

since y=S−1xy=S^{-1}xy=S−1x, consider y0=S−1x0y^0=S^{-1}x^0y0=S−1x0, then:

y1=y0−S−1α0(∇2f(Sy0))−1∇f(Sy0)y1=S−1x0−S−1α0(∇2f(x0))−1∇f(x0)y^{1}=y^0-S^{-1}\alpha^0(\nabla^2f(Sy^0))^{-1}\nabla f(Sy^0)\\ y^{1}=S^{-1}x^0-S^{-1}\alpha^0(\nabla^2f(x^0))^{-1}\nabla f(x^0) y1=y0−S−1α0(∇2f(Sy0))−1∇f(Sy0)y1=S−1x0−S−1α0(∇2f(x0))−1∇f(x0)

so y1=S−1x1y^1=S^{-1}x^1y1=S−1x1, for yk=S−1xky^k=S^{-1}x^kyk=S−1xk:

yk+1=yk−S−1αk(∇2f(Syk))−1∇f(Syk)yk+1=S−1xk−S−1αk(∇2f(Syk))−1∇f(Syk)yk+1=S−1xk+1y^{k+1}=y^k-S^{-1}\alpha^k(\nabla^2f(Sy^k))^{-1}\nabla f(Sy^k)\\ y^{k+1}=S^{-1}x^k-S^{-1}\alpha^k(\nabla^2f(Sy^k))^{-1}\nabla f(Sy^k)\\ y^{k+1}=S^{-1}x^{k+1} yk+1=yk−S−1αk(∇2f(Syk))−1∇f(Syk)yk+1=S−1xk−S−1αk(∇2f(Syk))−1∇f(Syk)yk+1=S−1xk+1

summarizing, the sequence made by Newton’s Method yk=S−1xky^k=S^{-1}x^kyk=S−1xk.

(8)

PROOF:

Since f(x)f(x)f(x) is convex,

∀x1,x2∈Ω,λ∈(0,1)λf(x1)+(1−λ)f(x2)≥f(λx1+(1−λ)x2)\forall x_1,x_2\in \Omega,\lambda\in(0,1)\quad \lambda f(x_1)+(1-\lambda )f(x_2)\geq f(\lambda x_1+(1-\lambda )x_2) ∀x1​,x2​∈Ω,λ∈(0,1)λf(x1​)+(1−λ)f(x2​)≥f(λx1​+(1−λ)x2​)

Since γ(x)\gamma(x)γ(x) is a monotone nondecreasing function,

γ(λf(x1)+(1−λ)f(x2))≥γ(f(λx1+(1−λ)x2))\gamma(\lambda f(x_1)+(1-\lambda )f(x_2))\geq \gamma (f(\lambda x_1+(1-\lambda )x_2)) γ(λf(x1​)+(1−λ)f(x2​))≥γ(f(λx1​+(1−λ)x2​))

Since γ(x)\gamma(x)γ(x) is convex,

λγ(f(x1))+(1−λ)γ(f(x2))≥γ(λf(x1)+(1−λ)f(x2))\lambda\gamma( f(x_1))+(1-\lambda )\gamma(f(x_2)) \geq \gamma(\lambda f(x_1)+(1-\lambda )f(x_2)) λγ(f(x1​))+(1−λ)γ(f(x2​))≥γ(λf(x1​)+(1−λ)f(x2​))

Therefore,

λγ(f(x1))+(1−λ)γ(f(x2))≥γ(f(λx1+(1−λ)x2))\lambda\gamma( f(x_1))+(1-\lambda )\gamma(f(x_2)) \geq \gamma (f(\lambda x_1+(1-\lambda )x_2)) λγ(f(x1​))+(1−λ)γ(f(x2​))≥γ(f(λx1​+(1−λ)x2​))

This implies that γ(f)(x)\gamma(f)(x)γ(f)(x) is convex.

(a)

f(x,y)=5x2+5y2−xy−11x+11y+11∇f(x,y)=(10x−y−11,10y−x+11)f(x,y)=5x^2+5y^2-xy-11x+11y+11\\\\ \nabla f(x,y)=(10x-y-11,10y-x+11) f(x,y)=5x2+5y2−xy−11x+11y+11∇f(x,y)=(10x−y−11,10y−x+11)

let\left\{ \begin{array}{**lr**} 10x-y-11=0\\ 10y-x+11=0 \end{array} \right.

the solution of the equation set above is (1,−1)(1,-1)(1,−1),

it is the point satisfying the first-order necessary conditions

∇2f(x,y)=(10−1−110)\nabla^2 f(x,y)= \begin{pmatrix} 10&-1\\ -1&10 \end{pmatrix} ∇2f(x,y)=(10−1​−110​)

it is easy to know the Hessian Matrix is always positive definite, so the point(1,−1)(1,-1)(1,−1) is a global minimum point, and the global minimum is 0

©

for the steepest decent method, Dk=ID^k=IDk=I

(Dk)12∇2f(x,y)(Dk)12=∇2f(x,y)=(10−1−110)(D^k)^{\frac12}\nabla^2f(x,y)(D^k)^{\frac12}=\nabla^2f(x,y)=\begin{pmatrix} 10&-1\\ -1&10 \end{pmatrix} (Dk)21​∇2f(x,y)(Dk)21​=∇2f(x,y)=(10−1​−110​)

(10)

(a)

f(x,y)=x2+y2+xy−3x∇f(x,y)=(2x+y−3,x+2y)∇2f(x,y)=(2112)f(x,y)=x^2+y^2+xy-3x\\\\ \nabla f(x,y)=(2x+y-3,x+2y)\\\\ \nabla^2f(x,y)= \begin{pmatrix} 2&1\\ 1&2 \end{pmatrix} f(x,y)=x2+y2+xy−3x∇f(x,y)=(2x+y−3,x+2y)∇2f(x,y)=(21​12​)

let\left\{ \begin{array}{**lr**} 2x+y-3=0\\ x+2y=0 \end{array} \right.

the solution of the equation set above is (2,−1)(2,-1)(2,−1), and the Hessian Matrix is always positive definite,

so point (2,−1)(2,-1)(2,−1) is unconstrained local minimum point.

(b)

Because Hessian Matrix is always positive definite, the function f(x,y)f(x,y)f(x,y) is convex for all point in the real space.

©

the minimum points must on the boundary of the constraint.

consider x=0x=0x=0, then f(x,y)=y2f(x,y)=y^2f(x,y)=y2, the minimum point is (0,0)(0,0)(0,0) and the minimum is 000

consider y=0y=0y=0, then f(x,y)=x2−3xf(x,y)=x^2-3xf(x,y)=x2−3x, the minimum point is (32,0)(\frac32,0)(23​,0) and the minimum is −94-\frac94−49​

therefore, the minimum point of f subject to x≥0,y≥0x\geq 0,y\geq 0x≥0,y≥0 is (32,0)(\frac32,0)(23​,0) and the minimum is −94-\frac94−49​

(d)

for the steepest decent method, Dk=ID^k=IDk=I

(Dk)12∇2f(x,y)(Dk)12=∇2f(x,y)=(2112)(D^k)^{\frac12}\nabla^2f(x,y)(D^k)^{\frac12}=\nabla^2f(x,y)=\begin{pmatrix} 2&1\\ 1&2 \end{pmatrix} (Dk)21​∇2f(x,y)(Dk)21​=∇2f(x,y)=(21​12​)

M=3,m=1f(xk+1)−f(x∗)f(xk)−f(x∗)≤(M−mM+m)2=14M=3,\quad m=1\\ \frac{f(x^{k+1})-f(x^*)}{f(x^{k})-f(x^*)}\leq(\frac{M-m}{M+m})^2=\frac14 M=3,m=1f(xk)−f(x∗)f(xk+1)−f(x∗)​≤(M+mM−m​)2=41​

Assignment 2

(1)

Q: Consider the quadratic program

min 0.5xTQx−bTxs.t. Ax=cmin\ 0.5x^TQx-b^Tx\\ s.t.\ Ax=c min 0.5xTQx−bTxs.t. Ax=c

Prove that x∗x^*x∗ is a local minimum point if and only if it is a global minimum point.(No convexity is assumed)

Proof:

Since no convexity is assumed, we consider two cases:

a) If Q is a positive definitive matrix. It’s obvious that x∗x^*x∗ is a local minimum point if and only if it is a global minimum point.

b) If Q is not a positive definitive matrix.

Assume x∗x^*x∗ is a local minimum point, set x∗+dx^*+dx∗+d as the global minimum point.

so A(x∗+d)=cA(x^*+d)=cA(x∗+d)=c

and because A(x∗)=cA(x^*)=cA(x∗)=c

so Ad=0Ad=0Ad=0

so we have A(x∗+2d)=cA(x*+2d)=cA(x∗+2d)=c

then according to the optimality condition, we have

∇f(x∗+d)′((x∗+2d)−(x∗+d))≥0(Q(x∗+d)−b′)d≥0\nabla f(x^*+d)'((x^*+2d)-(x^*+d))\geq0\\ (Q(x^*+d)-b')d\geq0 ∇f(x∗+d)′((x∗+2d)−(x∗+d))≥0(Q(x∗+d)−b′)d≥0

and we have

\begin{align} f(x^*+d)&=0.5(x^*+d)'Q(x^*+d)-b'(x^*+d)\\ &=0.5x^{*'}Qx^*-bx^*+(Q(x^*+d)-b')d-0.5d'Qd\\ &=f(x^*)+(Q(x^*+d)-b')d-0.5d'Qd\\ \end{align}

as (Q(x∗+d)−b′)d≥0(Q(x^*+d)-b')d\geq0(Q(x∗+d)−b′)d≥0 and −0.5d′Qd≥0-0.5d'Qd\geq0−0.5d′Qd≥0

then f(x∗+d)≥f(x∗)f(x^*+d)\geq f(x^*)f(x∗+d)≥f(x∗)

and because f(x∗+d)f(x^*+d)f(x∗+d) is the global minimum, so we can get that f(x∗+d)=f(x∗)f(x^*+d)=f(x^*)f(x∗+d)=f(x∗). and then we can conclude that the local minimum is the global minimum.

(2)

Suppose that the projected negative nablaient d is calculated satisfying

−g=d+AqTλ-g=d+A_q^T\lambda −g=d+AqT​λ

and that some component λi\lambda_iλi​ of λ\lambdaλ, corresponding to an inequality, is negative. Show that if the iiith inequality is dropped, the projection did_idi​ of the negative nablaient onto the remaining constraints is a feasible direction of decent

Proof:

Let the new direction vector did_idi​ by relaxing the iiith constraint and projecting the negative nablaient onto the subspace determined by remaining q−1q-1q−1 active constraints.

Let A_\bar q denote the matrix AqA_qAq​ with row aja_jaj​ deleted.

We know that gTdi<0g^Td_i<0gTdi​<0 .

Multiplying the equation -g=d_i+A^T_\bar q\lambda_i by did_idi​ and using A_\bar q d_i=0 we obtain:

0>gTdi=−λiaiTdi0>g^Td_i=-\lambda_ia_i^Td_i 0>gTdi​=−λi​aiT​di​

Since λi<0\lambda_i<0λi​<0 , we conclude that diTdi<0d_i^Td_i<0diT​di​<0 .

So the vector did_idi​ is a direction of descent, but it is a feasible direction.

(3) 3.1.9

(a)

min x+y+zs.t.xyz=ρ2(x+y+z)min\ x+y+z\\ s.t. xyz=\rho^2(x+y+z) min x+y+zs.t.xyz=ρ2(x+y+z)

consider the Lagrangian Relaxation:

L(x,y,z,λ)=x+y+z+λ(xyz−ρ2(x+y+z))L(x,y,z,\lambda)=x+y+z+\lambda(xyz-\rho^2(x+y+z)) L(x,y,z,λ)=x+y+z+λ(xyz−ρ2(x+y+z))

let ∇L=0\nabla L=0∇L=0, then we have

\left\{ \begin{array}{**lr**} 1+\lambda(yz-\rho^2)=0\\ 1+\lambda(xz-\rho^2)=0\\ 1+\lambda(xy-\rho^2)=0\\ xyz=\rho^2(x+y+z) \end{array} \right.

then we have:

y+zx=x+zy=x+yz\frac{y+z}x=\frac{x+z}y=\frac{x+y}z xy+z​=yx+z​=zx+y​

so x=y=zx=y=zx=y=z

(b)

consider all lines parallel to the line that passes through aaa and bbb and

intersect the circle.

the maximal area appears only if the point xxx is the point of tangency between the parallel line and the circle.

and we can see that the line through xxx and x^\hat xx^ is also the line perpendicular to the tangent line and through point xxx.

so this line must passes through the center of the circle.

©

Fix two vertices and the corresponding side of the corresponding side of the triangle and optimize vertex and the vertex of the circle should be orthogonal to the fixed side. Hence, the optimal is obtained when the two remaining side are equal. Repeating this argument, fixing each side of the triangle in turn, we see that all sides should be equal.

(d)

Similar line of argument as in ©.

(4) 3.3.5

(a)

Let d∈Fˉ(x∗)d\in \bar F(x^*)d∈Fˉ(x∗), so ∃{dk}⊂F(x∗)\exists \{d^k\}\subset F(x^*)∃{dk}⊂F(x∗), so that dk→dd^k\rightarrow ddk→d.

And we have:

∇f(x∗)′dk=limα→0f(x∗+αdk)−f(x∗)α\nabla f(x^*)'d^k=lim_{\alpha\rightarrow0}\frac{f(x^*+\alpha d^k)-f(x^*)}\alpha ∇f(x∗)′dk=limα→0​αf(x∗+αdk)−f(x∗)​

Because x∗x^*x∗ is a constrained local minimum, sof(x∗+αdk)−f(x∗)α≥0\frac{f(x^*+\alpha d^k)-f(x^*)}\alpha\geq0αf(x∗+αdk)−f(x∗)​≥0 .

So, ∇f(x∗)′dk≥0\nabla f(x^*)'d^k\geq0∇f(x∗)′dk≥0.

(b)

If x* is a constrained local minimum, from (a) we can get

∇f(x∗)′dk≥0, ∀d with ∇gj(x∗)′d≤0, ∀j∈A(x∗)\nabla f(x^*)'d^k\geq0,\ \forall d\ with\ \nabla g_j(x^*)'d\leq0,\ \forall j\in A(x^*) ∇f(x∗)′dk≥0, ∀d with ∇gj​(x∗)′d≤0, ∀j∈A(x∗)

According to Farkas’Lemma, it is true iff there exists u∗u^*u∗ such that

−∇f(x∗)=∑j∈A(x∗)uj∇gj(x∗), uj∗≥0-\nabla f(x^*)=\sum_{j\in A(x^*)}u_j\nabla g_j(x^*),\ u_j^*\geq0 −∇f(x∗)=j∈A(x∗)∑​uj​∇gj​(x∗), uj∗​≥0

Setting uj∗=0u_j^*=0uj∗​=0 for j∉A(x∗)j\notin A(x^*)j∈/​A(x∗) .

we have the desired result.

©

We want to show that Fˉ(x∗)=V(x)\bar F(x^*)=V(x)Fˉ(x∗)=V(x), where V(x∗)V(x^*)V(x∗) is the cone of first order feasible variations given by V(x∗)={d∣∇gj(x∗)′d≤0, ∀j∈A(x∗}V(x^*)=\{d|\nabla g_j(x^*)'d\leq0,\ \forall j\in A(x^*\}V(x∗)={d∣∇gj​(x∗)′d≤0, ∀j∈A(x∗}.

From Mean Value Theorem, for each j∈A(x∗)j \in A(x^*)j∈A(x∗) and for any d∈F(x∗)d\in F(x^*)d∈F(x∗) there exist ε∈[0,1]\varepsilon\in[0,1]ε∈[0,1] such that gj(x∗+αd)=gj(x∗)+α∇gj(x∗+εαd)′dg_j(x^*+\alpha d)=g_j(x^*)+\alpha\nabla g_j(x^*+\varepsilon\alpha d)'dgj​(x∗+αd)=gj​(x∗)+α∇gj​(x∗+εαd)′d.

Because gj(x∗+αd)≤0g_j(x^*+\alpha d)\leq0gj​(x∗+αd)≤0, so

lim⁡α→0α∇gj(x∗+εαd)′d≤0\lim_{\alpha\rightarrow0}\alpha\nabla g_j(x^*+\varepsilon\alpha d)'d\leq0 α→0lim​α∇gj​(x∗+εαd)′d≤0

And that implies that $g_j(x^)d\leq0,\forall j\in A(x^ $, so that d∈V(x∗)d\in V(x^*)d∈V(x∗). Therefore F(x∗)⊂V(x)F(x^*)\subset V(x)F(x∗)⊂V(x), since V(x∗)V(x^*)V(x∗) is closed, so Fˉ(x∗)⊂V(x)\bar F(x^*)\subset V(x)Fˉ(x∗)⊂V(x).

From each of 1-4, we can also prove that Fˉ(x∗)⊃V(x)\bar F(x^*)\supset V(x)Fˉ(x∗)⊃V(x).

So we can get that $ \bar F(x^*)=V(x)$.

(d)

We can easily get the local minimum is x∗=(0,0)x^*= (0,0)x∗=(0,0). So we have

∇g1(0,0)=(0,1)T∇g2(0,0)=(0,−1)T\nabla g_1(0,0)=(0,1)^T\\ \nabla g_2(0,0)=(0,-1)^T ∇g1​(0,0)=(0,1)T∇g2​(0,0)=(0,−1)T

Obviously g1 and g2 are not linear, so the condition(1) is violated. Furthermore, there is no vector d=(d1,d2)’d= (d1,d2)’d=(d1,d2)’ such that

∇g1(0,0)′d=d2<0, ∇g1(0,0)′d=−d2<0\nabla g_1(0,0)'d=d_2<0,\ \nabla g_1(0,0)'d=-d_2<0 ∇g1​(0,0)′d=d2​<0, ∇g1​(0,0)′d=−d2​<0

So the condition2 is violated. Similarly, condition3 is violated. The vectors ∇g1(0,0)\nabla g_1(0,0)∇g1​(0,0) and ∇g2(0,0)\nabla g_2(0,0)∇g2​(0,0) are linearly dependent since ∇g1(0,0)=−∇g2(0,0)\nabla g_1(0,0)=-\nabla g_2(0,0)∇g1​(0,0)=−∇g2​(0,0), so the condition4 is violated.

Assume u0∇f(x∗)+u1∇g1(x∗)+u2∇g2(x∗)=0u_0\nabla f(x^*)+u_1\nabla g_1(x^*)+u_2\nabla g_2(x^*)=0u0​∇f(x∗)+u1​∇g1​(x∗)+u2​∇g2​(x∗)=0, so we have:

(u0,u0)′+(0,u1)′+(0,u2)′=(0,0)′(u_0,u_0)'+(0,u_1)'+(0,u_2)'=(0,0)' (u0​,u0​)′+(0,u1​)′+(0,u2​)′=(0,0)′

It follows that u0=0u_0=0u0​=0. So there is no Lagrange multiplier.

(e)

From the description we know that x∗x^*x∗ is also a local minimum for the modified problem.

The modified problem has a single constraint g1(x)=∣∣h(x)∣∣2g_1(x)=||h(x)||^2g1​(x)=∣∣h(x)∣∣2, which is active at x∗x^*x∗.

Since g1g_1g1​ is not linear, the condition 1 is violated.

Because ∇g1(x∗)=2∇h(x∗)∇h(x∗)=0\nabla g_1(x^*)=2\nabla h(x^*)\nabla h(x^*)=0∇g1​(x∗)=2∇h(x∗)∇h(x∗)=0, the conditions 2 and 4 are violated. If condition 3 holds, we can prove that condition 2 is hold too, so condition 3 is violated, too. From u0∗∇g(x∗=−u1∗∇g1(x∗))u_0^*\nabla g(x^*=-u_1^*\nabla g_1(x^*))u0∗​∇g(x∗=−u1∗​∇g1​(x∗)) and ∇g1(x∗)=0\nabla g_1(x^*)=0∇g1​(x∗)=0, it follows that u0∗=0u_0^*=0u0∗​=0, so there is no Lagrange multiplier.

(5) 5.1.7

(a)

let \ x_i=\left\{ \begin{array}{**lr**} 0 & if\ the\ object\ is\ included\ in\ the\ subset\\ 1 & otherwise \end{array} \right.

Then the total weight and value of objects included is ∑iwixi\sum_iw_ix_i∑i​wi​xi​ and \sum_iv_ix_i, respectively, and the problem can be written as

max f(x)=∑i=1nvixis.t.∑i=1nwixi≤A,xi∈{0,1},i=1,...,n.max\ f(x)=\sum_{i=1}^nv_ix_i\\ s. t.\sum_{i=1}^nw_ix_i\leq A, xi ∈ \{0, 1\}, i= 1, . . . , n. max f(x)=i=1∑n​vi​xi​s.t.i=1∑n​wi​xi​≤A,xi∈{0,1},i=1,...,n.

(b)

Let f^(x)=−f(x)\hat f(x)=-f(x)f^​(x)=−f(x) and consider the equivalent problem of minimizing f^(x)\hat f(x)f^​(x) subject to the constraints given above. Then

L(x,μ)=−∑i=1nvixi+μ(∑i=1nwixi−A)L(x,\mu)=-\sum_{i=1}^nv_ix_i+\mu(\sum_{i=1}^nw_ix_i-A) L(x,μ)=−i=1∑n​vi​xi​+μ(i=1∑n​wi​xi​−A)

and

q^(u)=inf⁡xi∈{0,1}{∑i=1n(μwi−vi)xi−μA}\hat q(u)=\inf_{x_i\in\{0,1\}}\{\sum_{i=1}^n(\mu w_i-v_i)x_i-\mu A\} q^​(u)=xi​∈{0,1}inf​{i=1∑n​(μwi​−vi​)xi​−μA}

Note that the minimization above is a separable problem, and the minimum is attained at

\hat x_i(\mu)= \left\{ \begin{array}{**lr**} 0& if\ \mu<v_i/w_i\\ 1& if\ \mu>v_i/w_i\\ o\ or\ 1 & otherwise \end{array} \right.

Without loss of generality, assume that the objects are ordered such that:

v1w1≤v2w2≤...≤vnwn\frac{v_1}{w_1}\leq\frac{v_2}{w_2}\leq...\leq\frac{v_n}{w_n} w1​v1​​≤w2​v2​​≤...≤wn​vn​​

Whenμ∈(vj−1wj−1,vjwj]\mu\in(\frac{v_{j-1}}{w_{j-1}},\frac{v_j}{w_j}]μ∈(wj−1​vj−1​​,wj​vj​​] for some jjj with 1≤j≤n1≤j ≤n1≤j≤n, then x^i=1\hat x_i=1x^i​=1 for all i≥ji≥ji≥j.

And $ \hat x_i(\mu) = 0$ otherwise, and

q^(μ)=u(∑i=1nwi−A)−∑i=1nvi\hat q(\mu)=u(\sum_{i=1}^nw_i-A)-\sum_{i=1}^nv_i q^​(μ)=u(i=1∑n​wi​−A)−i=1∑n​vi​

From this relation, we see that, as μ increases, the slope of q^(u)\hat q(u)q^​(u) decreases from ∑i=1nwi−A\sum_{i=1}^nw_i-A∑i=1n​wi​−A to −A−A−A. Therefore, if $ \sum_{i=1}^nw_i-A> 0$, q^(u)\hat q(u)q^​(u) is maximized when the slope of the curve goes from positive to negative. In this case, the dual optimal value q^∗\hat q^*q^​∗ is attained at u∗=vi∗wi∗u^*=\frac{v_i^*}{w_i^*}u∗=wi∗​vi∗​​, wherei∗i^*i∗is the largest iii such that

w1+w2+...+wn≥Aw_1+w_2+...+w_n\geq A w1​+w2​+...+wn​≥A

If ∑i=1nwi−A≤0\sum_{i=1}^nw_i-A ≤ 0∑i=1n​wi​−A≤0, then the dual optimal value q^∗\hat q^*q^​∗is attained at u∗=0u^* = 0u∗=0.

©

Consider a relaxed version of the problem of minimizing f^\hat ff^​:

max fR(x)=−∑i=1nvixis.t.∑i=1nwixi≤A,xi∈[0,1],i=1,...,n.max\ f_R(x)=-\sum_{i=1}^nv_ix_i\\ s. t.\sum_{i=1}^nw_ix_i\leq A, xi ∈ [0, 1], i= 1, . . . , n. max fR​(x)=−i=1∑n​vi​xi​s.t.i=1∑n​wi​xi​≤A,xi∈[0,1],i=1,...,n.

Let fR∗f_R^*fR∗​ and qR∗q_R^*qR∗​ be the optimal values of the relaxed problem and its dual, respectively.

In the relaxed problem, the cost function is convex over RnR^nRn(in fact it is linear), and the constraint set is polyhedral.

Thus, according to Prop. 5.2.1, there is no duality gap, and fR∗=qR∗f_R^*=q_R^*fR∗​=qR∗​ . The dual function of the relaxed problem is:

qR(μ)=inf⁡xi∈{0,1}{∑i=1n(μwi−vi)xi−μA}q_R(\mu)=\inf_{x_i\in\{0,1\}}\{\sum_{i=1}^n(\mu w_i-v_i)x_i-\mu A\} qR​(μ)=xi​∈{0,1}inf​{i=1∑n​(μwi​−vi​)xi​−μA}

Again, qR(x)q_R(x)qR​(x)is separable and the minimum is attained at

\hat x_i(\mu)= \left\{ \begin{array}{**lr**} 0& if\ \mu<v_i/w_i\\ 1& if\ \mu>v_i/w_i\\ o\ or\ 1 & otherwise \end{array} \right.

Thus the solution is the same as the {0,1}\{0, 1\}{0,1} constrained problem for all iii with μ≠viwi\mu\neq\frac{v_i}{w_i}μ​=wi​vi​​. For iii withμ=viwi\mu=\frac{v_i}{w_i}μ=wi​vi​​, the value of xix_ixi​ is irrelevant to the dual function value. Therefore, q^(u)=qR(μ)\hat q(u)=q_R(\mu)q^​(u)=qR​(μ) for all μ, and thus q^∗=qR∗\hat q^*=q_R^*q^​∗=qR∗​.

Following Example 5.1.2, it can be seen that the optimal primal and dual solution pair (x∗,μ∗)(x^∗, μ^∗)(x∗,μ∗) of the relaxed problem satisfies

μ∗wi=vi, if 0<xi∗<1,μ∗wi≥vi, if xi∗=0,μ∗wi≤vi, if xi∗=1,μ∗ w_i = v_i ,\ if\ 0 <x_i^*< 1,\\ μ∗ w_i \geq v_i ,\ if\ x_i^*=0,\\ μ∗ w_i \leq v_i ,\ if\ x_i^*= 1, μ∗wi​=vi​, if 0<xi∗​<1,μ∗wi​≥vi​, if xi∗​=0,μ∗wi​≤vi​, if xi∗​=1,

In fact, it is straightforward to show that there is an optimal solution of the relaxed problem such that at most one xi∗x^*_ixi∗​ satisfies 0<xi∗<10 < x_i^* < 10<xi∗​<1. Consider a solution equivalent to this optimal solution with the exception that = 0 if 0 < < 1. This solution is clearly feasible for the {0, 1} constrained problem, so that we have

f^∗≤f^(x^)≤fR∗+max⁡i≤i≤nvi\hat f^*\leq \hat f(\hat x)\leq f_R^*+\max_{i\leq i\leq n}v_i f^​∗≤f^​(x^)≤fR∗​+i≤i≤nmax​vi​

Combining with earlier results,

f^∗≤qR∗+max⁡i≤i≤nvi=q^∗+max⁡i≤i≤nvi\hat f^*\leq q_R^*+\max_{i\leq i\leq n}v_i=\hat q^*+\max_{i\leq i\leq n}v_i f^​∗≤qR∗​+i≤i≤nmax​vi​=q^​∗+i≤i≤nmax​vi​

Since f^∗=−f\hat f^*=-ff^​∗=−f and q^∗=−q∗\hat q^*=-q^*q^​∗=−q∗ we have the desired result.

(d)

Since the object weights and values remain the same we have from © that 0≤q∗(k)−f∗(k)≤max⁡i≤i≤nvi0\leq q^*(k)-f^*(k)\leq \max_{i\leq i\leq n}v_i0≤q∗(k)−f∗(k)≤maxi≤i≤n​vi​. By including in the subset each replica of an object assembled in the optimal solution to the original problem, we see that f∗(k)≥kf∗f^*(k)\geq kf^*f∗(k)≥kf∗ . It then follows that

lim⁡k→∞(q∗(k)−f∗(k))=0\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}(q^*(k)-f^*(k))=0 k→∞lim​(q∗(k)−f∗(k))=0

Assignment 3

5.5.2

min  f(x)=10x1+3x2s.t.  5x1+x2≥4  x1,x2∈{0,1}min\ \ f(x)=10x_1+3x_2\\ s.t.\ \ 5x_1+x_2\geq4\ \ x_1,x_2\in\{0,1\} min  f(x)=10x1​+3x2​s.t.  5x1​+x2​≥4  x1​,x2​∈{0,1}

Using Lagrangian relaxiation:

the Lagrangian funcion is

L(x,μ)=10x1+3x2+μ(4−5x1−x2)=(10−5μ)x1+(3−μ)x2+4μL(x,\mu)=10x_1+3x_2+\mu(4-5x_1-x_2)=(10-5\mu)x_1+(3-\mu)x_2+4\mu L(x,μ)=10x1​+3x2​+μ(4−5x1​−x2​)=(10−5μ)x1​+(3−μ)x2​+4μ

the dual problem is:

max  q(μ)=min⁡x∈{0,1}L(x,μ)s.t.  μ≥0max\ \ q(\mu)=\min_{x\in\{0,1\}}L(x,\mu)\\ s.t.\ \ \mu\geq0 max  q(μ)=x∈{0,1}min​L(x,μ)s.t.  μ≥0

the solution is μ=2,q∗=8\mu=2, q^*=8μ=2,q∗=8

Using constraint relaxation:

the releaxed problem is:

min  f(x)=10x1+3x2s.t.  5x1+x2≥4  x1,x2∈[0,1]min\ \ f(x)=10x_1+3x_2\\ s.t.\ \ 5x_1+x_2\geq4\ \ x_1,x_2\in[0,1] min  f(x)=10x1​+3x2​s.t.  5x1​+x2​≥4  x1​,x2​∈[0,1]

(a) when x1=0x_1=0x1​=0, the prolem is:

min  f(x)=3x2s.t.  x2≥4  x2∈[0,1]min\ \ f(x)=3x_2\\ s.t.\ \ x_2\geq4\ \ x_2\in[0,1] min  f(x)=3x2​s.t.  x2​≥4  x2​∈[0,1]

this problem is infeasible.

(b) when x1=1x_1=1x1​=1, the problem is:

min  f(x)=10+3x2s.t.  5+x2≥4  x2∈[0,1]min\ \ f(x)=10+3x_2\\ s.t.\ \ 5+x_2\geq4\ \ x_2\in [0,1] min  f(x)=10+3x2​s.t.  5+x2​≥4  x2​∈[0,1]

when x2=0x_2=0x2​=0, the value of object function is minimization. so the solution is (1,0)(1,0)(1,0), it is also integer solution, so the point (1,0)(1,0)(1,0) is the optimal point of the primal problem, minimization is 10

so the lower bound of Lagrangian relaxiation is less than constraint relaxation.

5.5.10

(a)

\begin{align*} \bar q&=\inf_{x\in \bar X}\{f(x)+\sum_{j=0}^{\bar r}\mu_jg_j(x)\}\\ &=\inf_{\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ x\in X\\g_{\bar r+1}(x)\leq0...g_r(x)\leq0}\{f(x)+\sum_{j=1}^{\bar r}\mu_jg_j(x)\}\\ &\geq \inf_{\ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ \ x\in X\\g_{\bar r+1}(x)\leq0...g_r(x)\leq0}\{f(x)+\sum_{j=1}^{r}\mu_jg_j(x)\}\\ &\geq \inf_{x\in X}\{f(x)+\sum_{j=1}^{r}\mu_jg_j(x)\}\\ &=q \end{align*}

so we have qˉ∗≥q∗\bar q^*\geq q^*qˉ​∗≥q∗, and we have qˉ∗≤f∗\bar q^*\leq f^*qˉ​∗≤f∗ by the weak duality theorem, so q∗≤qˉ∗≤f∗q^*\leq\bar q^*\leq f^*q∗≤qˉ​∗≤f∗

(b)

if q∗=f∗q^*=f^*q∗=f∗, and we haveq∗≤qˉ∗≤f∗q^*\leq\bar q^*\leq f^*q∗≤qˉ​∗≤f∗

so we can get q∗=qˉ∗=f∗q^*=\bar q^*=f^*q∗=qˉ​∗=f∗

©

min  f(x)=e−x1x2s.t.  x1=0, x1−1≤0, x∈X={x∣x≥0}min\ \ f(x)=e^{-x_1x_2}\\ s.t.\ \ x_1=0,\ x_1-1\leq0,\ x\in X=\{x|x\geq0\} min  f(x)=e−x1​x2​s.t.  x1​=0, x1​−1≤0, x∈X={x∣x≥0}

Xˉ={x∣x≥0,x1=0}\bar X=\{x|x\geq0, x_1=0\} Xˉ={x∣x≥0,x1​=0}

and we can get q∗=0,qˉ∗=f∗=1q^*=0,\bar q^*=f^*=1q∗=0,qˉ​∗=f∗=1, so q∗<qˉ∗=f∗q^*<\bar q^*=f^*q∗<qˉ​∗=f∗

6.3.1

(a)

Let μ∗\mu^∗μ∗ be a dual optimal solution, we obtain:

∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2−2sk(q∗−q(uk))+(sk)2∣∣gk∣∣2||\mu^{k+1}-\mu^*||^2\leq ||\mu^{k}-\mu^*||^2-2s^k(q^*-q(u^k))+(s^k)^2||g^k||^2 ∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2−2sk(q∗−q(uk))+(sk)2∣∣gk∣∣2

where q∗=q(μ∗)q^*=q(\mu^*)q∗=q(μ∗), sk=q∗−q(μk)∣∣gk∣∣2s^k=\frac{q^*-q(\mu^k)}{||g^k||^2}sk=∣∣gk∣∣2q∗−q(μk)​, and we have:

∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2−q∗−q(μk)∣∣gk∣∣2||\mu^{k+1}-\mu^*||^2\leq ||\mu^{k}-\mu^*||^2-\frac{q^*-q(\mu^k)}{||g^k||^2} ∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2−∣∣gk∣∣2q∗−q(μk)​

So

∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2,  ∀k||\mu^{k+1}-\mu^*||^2\leq ||\mu^{k}-\mu^*||^2,\ \ \forall k ∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2,  ∀k

implying that {μk}\{\mu^k\}{μk} is bounded.

(b)

Let CCC be a positive constant such that ∣∣gk∣∣≤C||g^k|| ≤ C∣∣gk∣∣≤C for all kkk, then

∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2+q∗−q(μk)C2≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2||\mu^{k+1}-\mu^*||^2+\frac{q^*-q(\mu^k)}{C^2}\leq ||\mu^{k}-\mu^*||^2 ∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2+C2q∗−q(μk)​≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2

By summing these inequalities over all kkk, we obtain

1C2∑k=0∞(q∗−q(μk))2≤∣∣μ0−μ∗∣∣2\frac1{C^2}\sum_{k=0}^\infty(q^*-q(\mu^k))^2\leq||\mu^0-\mu^*||^2 C21​k=0∑∞​(q∗−q(μk))2≤∣∣μ0−μ∗∣∣2

so lim⁡k→∞q(μk)=q∗\lim_{k\rightarrow \infty}q(\mu^k)=q^*limk→∞​q(μk)=q∗

Since μk{\mu^k}μk is bounded, there exist a vector μ^\hat \muμ^​ and a subsequence ${\mu^k}_{k\in\mathcal K} \subset {\mu^k} $converging to μ^∈M\hat\mu ∈ Mμ^​∈M. By using the upper-semicontinuity of q, we have

lim⁡k→∞sup⁡k∈Kq(μk)≤q(μ^)≤q∗\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}\sup_{k\in\mathcal K}q(\mu^k)\leq q(\hat \mu)\leq q^* k→∞lim​k∈Ksup​q(μk)≤q(μ^​)≤q∗

Now we show that {μk}\{\mu^k\}{μk} actually converges. Let M∗M^∗M∗ denote the set of all dual optimal solutions. Note that M∗M^∗M∗ is convex (by concavity of qqq) and closed (by upper-semicontinuity of qqq). Suppose that {μk}\{\mu^k\}{μk} has two distinct limit points, say μ^∈M∗\hat\mu \in M^∗μ^​∈M∗ and μ~∈M∗\widetilde\mu \in M^∗μ​∈M∗. As seen in (a), for any μ∗∈M∗\mu^* \in M^∗μ∗∈M∗, the sequence {∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣}\{||\mu^k − \mu^∗||\}{∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣} decreases monotonically, and therefore it converges. Hence ∣∣μ^−μ∗∣∣=∣∣μ~−μ∗∣∣||\hat\mu − \mu^∗|| = ||\widetilde\mu − \mu^∗||∣∣μ^​−μ∗∣∣=∣∣μ​−μ∗∣∣ for all μ∗∈M∗\mu^* \in M^∗μ∗∈M∗, implying that μ^=μ~\hat \mu=\widetilde \muμ^​=μ​.

©

Let qqq be real-valued and concave over the entire space Rr\mathbb{R}^rRr. According to Prop. B.24 of Appendix B, since {μk}\{\mu^k\}{μk} is bounded, the set ∪k≥0∂q(μk)∪_{k≥0}∂q(\mu^k)∪k≥0​∂q(μk) is bounded, and so is {gk}\{g^k\}{gk}.

6.3.3

(a)

Suppose that lim⁡k→∞inf⁡k(q∗−q(μk))>0\lim_{k\rightarrow\infty}\inf\sqrt{k}(q^*-q(\mu^k))>0limk→∞​infk​(q∗−q(μk))>0, Then there is an ϵ>0\epsilon>0ϵ>0 and large enough kˉ\bar kkˉ such that k(q∗−q(μk))≥ϵ\sqrt{k}(q^*-q(\mu^k))\geq\epsilonk​(q∗−q(μk))≥ϵ for all k≥kˉk\geq \bar kk≥kˉ, so

(q∗−q(μk))2≥ϵ2k,    ∀k≥kˉ(q^*-q(\mu^k))^2\geq\frac{\epsilon^2}{k},\ \ \ \ \forall k\geq \bar k (q∗−q(μk))2≥kϵ2​,    ∀k≥kˉ

implying that

∑k=kˉ∞(q∗−q(μk))2≥ϵ2∑k=kˉ∞1k=∞\sum_{k=\bar k}^\infty(q^*-q(\mu^k))^2\geq \epsilon^2\sum_{k=\bar k}^\infty\frac1k=\infty k=kˉ∑∞​(q∗−q(μk))2≥ϵ2k=kˉ∑∞​k1​=∞

which contradicts the relation

∑k=0∞(q∗−q(μk))2<∞\sum_{k=0}^\infty(q^*-q(\mu^k))^2<\infty k=0∑∞​(q∗−q(μk))2<∞

(b)

As seen in 6.3.1, we have for all dual optimal solutions μ∗\mu^*μ∗ and all kkk

∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2−q∗−q(μk)∣∣gk∣∣2||\mu^{k+1}-\mu^*||^2\leq ||\mu^{k}-\mu^*||^2-\frac{q^*-q(\mu^k)}{||g^k||^2} ∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2−∣∣gk∣∣2q∗−q(μk)​

This relation and the inequality q(μ∗)−q(μk)≥a∣∣μ∗−μk∣∣q(\mu^∗) − q(\mu^k) ≥ a||\mu^∗ − \mu^k||q(μ∗)−q(μk)≥a∣∣μ∗−μk∣∣ yield for all kkk

∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2−a2∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2∣∣gk∣∣2||\mu^{k+1}-\mu^*||^2\leq ||\mu^{k}-\mu^*||^2-\frac{a^2||\mu^k-\mu^*||^2}{||g^k||^2} ∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2−∣∣gk∣∣2a2∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2​

from which, by using sup⁡k≥0∣∣gk∣∣≤b\sup_{k≥0} ||g^k||≤ bsupk≥0​∣∣gk∣∣≤b, we obtain

∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤(1−a2b2)∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2||\mu^{k+1}-\mu^*||^2\leq (1-\frac{a^2}{b^2})||\mu^{k}-\mu^*||^2 ∣∣μk+1−μ∗∣∣2≤(1−b2a2​)∣∣μk−μ∗∣∣2

and the desired relation follows.

Assignment 4

2.1

(a)

(b)

We cloud use dynamic program to solve this question, the computational process is following:

and the makespan of this project is 39 weeks

©

This is the most cost trace of the project, shorting each of them could save a week time.

job 7 (Walls) should be shorten most since it cost 10 weeks and all the following work must wait for its finishing.

2.4

(a)

The processor sharing is a service discipline where a number of jobs are processed simultaneously, it can be seen that the processing could be interrupt any time. For CPU, the processing already done on the preempted job is not lost, so the model of the processor sharing concept on CPU is Preemptive-Resume form.

(b)

Assume that the switch of job takes no time, there are nnn jobs need to be processed, each job has a weight wjw_jwj​ and process time cjc_jcj​. Function g(j,t)g(j,t)g(j,t) shows if the job jjj has been finished on time ttt, if finished, g(j,t)=0g(j,t)=0g(j,t)=0; else g(j,t)=1g(j,t)=1g(j,t)=1. Function f(j,t)=1f(j,t)=1f(j,t)=1 shows that job jjj are processed on time t, f(j,t)=0f(j,t)=0f(j,t)=0 shows that job jjj are not processed on time t

We would like the cpu process the job with high weight as quickly as possible, so we get the following objective optimization problem:

min\ \ \sum_{j=1}^n\int_0^{+\infty}g(j,t)w_j\\ s.t.\ \ \sum_{j=1}^nf(j,t)\leq 1\\ \ \ \ \ g(j,t)=\left\{ \begin{array}{**lr**} 1 & if \int_0^tf(j,\tau)d\tau\geq c_j\\ 0 & otherwise \end{array} \right.\\ \int_0^{+\infty}f(j,\tau)d\tau=c_j

2.9

jobs 1 2 3 4
p1jp_{1j}p1j​ 8 6 4 12
p2jp_{2j}p2j​ 4 9 10 6

Consider the instance of O2 || Cmax with 4 jobs. The processing times of the four jobs on the two machines are above. Find all optimal schedules and compute the makespan under an optimal schedule.

we could easily find that the sum of processing time of job 1 and job 4 on machine 1 is similiar as the sum of processing time of job 2 and job 3 on machine 2; and the sum of processing time of job 1 and job 4 on machine 21 is same as the sum of processing time of job 2 and job 3 on machine 1. so all the optimial schedule is following:

machine 1 machine 2
1->4->2->3 2->3->1->4
1->4->2->3 3->2->1->4
4->1->2->3 2->3->4->1
4->1->2->3 3->2->4->1
(2->3 or 3->2)->(1->4 or 4->1) (1->4 or 4->1)->(2->3 or 3->2)

and the minimum cost time is 30

a

Find the optimal sequence for the following 1∣∣∑iwiTi1|| \sum_iw_iT_i1∣∣∑i​wi​Ti​ problem:

Jobs 1 2 3 4 5 6 7

pi 6 18 12 10 10 17 16

wi 1 5 2 4 1 4 2

di 8 42 44 24 90 85 68

For this problem, Ti=max(Ci−di,0)T_i=max(C_i-d_i,0)Ti​=max(Ci​−di​,0), if we would like to minimum ∑iwiTi\sum_iw_iT_i∑i​wi​Ti​, we should try to make Ci<diC_i<d_iCi​<di​.

so we firstly choose the job 1 and job 4, the sequence now is 1->4, then the problem change to:

jobs 2 3 5 6 7
pip_ipi​ 18 12 10 17 16
wiw_iwi​ 5 2 1 4 2
did_idi​ 26 28 74 69 52

then we choose job 2 and job 3, and we can see that w2=5>w3=1w_2=5>w_3=1w2​=5>w3​=1 and p2+p3=30>28>26p_2+p_3=30>28>26p2​+p3​=30>28>26. In order to less the objective function, job 2 should be done befor job 3, so the sequence now is 1->4->2->3, the problem change to:

jobs 5 6 7
pip_ipi​ 10 17 16
wiw_iwi​ 1 4 2
did_idi​ 44 39 22

then we could get the fianl sequence: 1->4->2->3->7->6->5。

and the minimum of the objective funciton ∑iwiTi=4\sum_iw_iT_{i}=4∑i​wi​Ti​=4

b

For the above problem, what is the optimal schedule if there are two identical machines?

the schedual changes to:

machine 1: 1->2->7->5

machine 2: 4->3->6

c

In applying Lagrangian relaxation to parallel machine scheduling problems, what will happen when ALL the parts are identical, i.e., have the same processing time and the same due date? Examine the problem from both the primal aspect and the dual aspect. What can be done to improve algorithm performance?

We can separate the problem to sub-problems, no matter in primal or dual aspect, if all the parts are identical. We also can consider all the parts as a whole and so do the machines and simplify the parallel-machine problem to one machine problem.
